course_description:
Enrolment Note:This course has maximum quota of 25 students per semester, seminars with less than 10 students may not run. Enrolment for Seminars opens in O Week following the Honours Orientation information session. Enrolment will close once the quota has been reached. This course provides students with the opportunity to gain in-depth knowledge of the primary research literature within the context of the wider theoretical framework in relation to relevant topics that vary from year to year.

Are our moral judgments based on rational thinking or intuitions? How do our emotions influence our moral judgments? And how does morality shape our social cognition and cultural norms? In this seminar, we will be exploring the field of moral psychology and its application to understanding human moral behaviour. We will begin by examining the theories of moral reasoning, moral intuitions, moral emotions, and moral character. In the applied part of the seminar, we will consider practical, real-world issues through the lens of moral psychology. We will examine our moral intuitions with regards to animals, the moral implications of aesthetic judgements, and the moral and ethical considerations of climate change. By the end of the seminar, students can expect to gain a better understanding of the psychological foundations of morality and its impact on our everyday decisions and actions. The seminar will also foster communication skills and the ability to critically evaluate psychological literature.

restrictions: BPsySc(Hons), BSc(Hons) in Psychology, BA(Hons) in Psychology students only.

course_aims: This course aims to develop skills in critical evaluation and to deepen understanding of contemporary research on a topic in psychology.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a critical and in-depth understanding of key topics in an area of psychology.
Demonstrate an ability to communicate orally and in writing about a topic in an area of psychology.
Demonstrate an understanding of how different types of research methods can contribute to our understanding of an area of psychology.
Effectively engage with others to discuss and evaluate empirical research.

assessment_summary.title: Infographic: Synthesis of a psychological topic
Student Presentation
assessment_summary.due_date: Due the week you are assigned in the first two weeks of class

assessment_details.title: Infographic: Synthesis of a psychological topic
assessment_details.task_description: Each student is required to produce an infographic based on the same paper that you present in class. The infographic should communicate the core ideas, findings, and contribution of the paper in a clear and visually engaging way, suitable for a non-specialist audience. The aim of the infographic is not to reproduce the paper in full, but to distil its key arguments and evidence and present them in a concise, accessible format. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Infographic will be submitted to the Turnitin link on the course Blackboard Page
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
For full details please review the School of Psychology Student Guidelines - Extensions and Deferred quiz/exam (the web link can be found on the Policies and Procedures tab).
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Student Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Due the week you are assigned in the first two weeks of class
assessment_details.task_description: Every week, two or three students will present one paper each to the class. Each presentation should be around 25 minutes, with an additional 15 minutes for questions and discussion. Each student will present one paper during the semester. These papers have been chosen because they are interesting and can advance our thinking about a particular topic. The goal of the presentation is to provide an overview and analysis of the assigned paper, and to stimulate class discussion. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If you are unable to complete your presentation you should submit appropriate documentation (as per the extension policy in the School of Psychology Student Guidelines) to your Course Coordinator to arrange for an alternate time to complete your presentation. You need to submit this documentation before the scheduled presentation date.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Student Participation
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students are expected to attend all presentations and come prepared to discuss the topic at hand. You are expected to actively contribute to the discussions, ask the speaker constructive and considered questions, demonstrate in your comments and questions that you clearly understand the topics that we’ve discussed. To be able to participate in this way it is necessary to adequately prepare for each session. Questions from your assigned reading need to be emailed through to the course coordinator by 5pm on the day before the class. Questions received after 10am will not be accepted to count towards your preparation mark. Read full task description Hide full task description

You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic: Introducing PSYC4886 An introduction to the course content and structure.
Moral intuitions More introductory discussion on the topics covered in the remaining weeks of the course.
Dyadic morality Discussion of the basic methods of investigation in the field
Moral foundations theory Lecturer and students will present and critically evaluate research papers. Class members will debate and discuss any aspect of the paper or related ideas.
Moral emotions Lecturer and students will present and critically evaluate research papers. Class members will debate and discuss any aspect of the paper or related ideas.
Moral character Lecturer and students will present and critically evaluate research papers. Class members will debate and discuss any aspect of the paper or related ideas.
Moral expansiveness Lecturer and students will present and critically evaluate research papers. Class members will debate and discuss any aspect of the paper or related ideas.
Thinking morally about animals Lecturer and students will present and critically evaluate research papers. Class members will debate and discuss any aspect of the paper or related ideas.
Beyond mind perception Lecturer and students will present and critically evaluate research papers. Class members will debate and discuss any aspect of the paper or related ideas.
Impression formation Students will have the chance to hear from current PhD students in the lab, who will present some of their work in visual neuroscience.
Moral conviction Poster session where students will present a thought experiment in the area covered by the seminar.
Climate change The lecturer will sum up and discuss the main topics of the seminar. Class members will debate and discuss any aspect of a target paper or related ideas.
